title: Least Privilege for Incident Tools
summary: Least privilege means granting the managed identity only the incident-system actions it needs: read incidents, read on-call schedules, add discussion, acknowledge, create, update, or resolve.
analogy: A hospital volunteer may read a room number, a nurse may update vitals, and a doctor may discharge the patient. All have badges, but badges do not grant identical powers.
diagram:
graph TD
ReadOnly[Read only identity] --> Query[query incidents]
ReadOnly --> GetOnCall[get on call]
Writer[Write identity] --> AddDiscussion[add discussion]
Writer --> Acknowledge[acknowledge]
Commander[Commander identity] --> Resolve[resolve incident]
body:
## First principles

First principles: identity without least privilege is just a safer way to do too much damage. An incident-reading agent should not be able to create, acknowledge, or resolve incidents unless that is explicitly required and approved.

## Definition

Least privilege means granting the managed identity only the incident-system actions it needs: read incidents, read on-call schedules, add discussion, acknowledge, create, update, or resolve. Separate read-only and write-capable agents or identities when possible.

## How it fits together

Build a permission matrix before writing code. Map each MCP tool to the minimum role. Prefer read-only by default; put create, acknowledge, update, and resolve behind approval and stronger roles. Audit every write with identity, arguments, and approval record.

## Worked example

```python
# Representative policy matrix. Enforce this in the MCP server before backend calls.
TOOL_ROLES = {
    "query_incidents": {"Incident.Reader"},
    "get_incident": {"Incident.Reader"},
    "get_on_call": {"Incident.Reader"},
    "add_discussion": {"Incident.Commenter"},
    "acknowledge_incident": {"Incident.Responder"},
    "create_incident": {"Incident.Creator"},
    "update_incident": {"Incident.Responder"},
}

def allowed(tool_name: str, caller_roles: set[str]) -> bool:
    return bool(TOOL_ROLES.get(tool_name, set()) & caller_roles)

print(allowed("query_incidents", {"Incident.Reader"}))
print(allowed("acknowledge_incident", {"Incident.Reader"}))
```

*Representative RBAC gate for MCP incident tools*

drills.q: Why separate read and write roles?
drills.a: So normal question answering cannot accidentally mutate operational records.
create, acknowledge, update, resolve, and any discussion or annotation write.

drills.q: Where should the permission check run?
drills.a: In the MCP server and backend authorization layer, not only in the agent prompt.

teachItBack: Managed identity must be paired with least privilege. Give read tools read roles and write tools only to identities that truly need them. Approval protects user intent, while RBAC protects the backend regardless of prompt behavior.

glossary.definition: Role-based access control, where permissions are grouped into roles assigned to identities.
Grant only the minimum permissions required for the task.
A table mapping tools or actions to required roles.
